sentences.text: My name is now Boris Abel.
Before I was in Lithuanian, Berelis Abelskis.
I'm born in 1915, July 23, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Panevezys.
Lithuania.
The name of my father?
My-- first-- my father's name?
My father's name is [BACKROUND NOISE Schneir Benjamin Abelskis.
My mother's name is [BACKROUND NOISE] Sonya Abelskis.
My father was a rope and twine manufacturer.
He established this in 1902.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On-- after the war, [BACKROUND NOISE] when his children grew up, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was the company larger, you know.
Larger.
And after this, when I grew up, when I was [BACKROUND NOISE] maybe 18 years old, we made a corporation.
And the family was the owners from the corporation.
And we, we had branches in the capital cities of Lithuania.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we used to import all kind stuff from all over the world.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [BACKROUND NOISE] It's a big company.
One, yeah.
My father come over because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the war, he had to come in.
And during the war, he was-- no, but when the revolution was, he was in in Russia.
And after this, he come over back to Lithuania.
Yeah.
I had four brothers and two sisters.
Number [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] six.
Yeah.
My father was religious, yeah.
Family was religious, yeah.
Yeah.
He was religious.
He was-- he even built a synagogue by himself.
And he was the president of the synagogue.
And he was the cantor of the synagogue.
And he was the treasurer of the synagogue.
And he was the supporter of the synagogue.
Was a lot of them, but was a lot of synagogues.
Although my father had to have, like, the Hasidic-- a Hasidic synagogue that-- he established this.
Yeah, my father-- my mother was going according to--
--the whole family had to--
--go, you know, a little bit [LAUGHS] in the background.
[LAUGHTER]
All, all holidays-- we observe all the holidays, especially the holidays where we used to be-- Easter and the High Holidays.
Passover and the High Holidays, Yom Kippur and New Year, see?
This holiday, when we grew up and my brothers and sisters was married with children, that we used to come in all together in one place in Panevezys.
And we used to celebrate the holidays all together.
And all our stores all over the country used to be closed.
And they used to come in all together to be in one place.
Yes.
I was in this time as not a small kid.
But I liked it, you know?
We used to wait for that, you know, because it's not like today, you know.
We used to go-- maybe two months before the holidays, my mother used to prepare everything, you know.
[BACKROUND NOISE] You couldn't go in the grocer and just buy the stuff, you know.
They, they used to make the-- everything-- prepared to have everything for that.
Who is that?
Matzah-- we bought matzah.
We used to buy it.
But this same-- a long time before holiday, we used to buy all the matzah should be-- for sure we have the matzah--
--in case they will run out.
[LAUGHS]
We had-- the house was-- the-- our factory's a rope, rope factory.
We had a lot of land, you know.
And we used to live in the-- in the same place where the factory is.
And we had two houses there.
And after this, we build that big house at [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
In the same place.
Same place, yeah.
Yeah.
When, when we build the house, was flowing water, you know?
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everything what we got like today, you know.
Yeah, because not everybody had it, you know.
Before even the-- before, people didn't even-- when we used to have a well, you know, for takes out water.
We had two of them.
And the whole-- the whole streets used to come in.
We used to give them the water free, you know, because not everybody could afford to have a well.
Well, after this, when we-- was more modern, we built already with motors.
We used to, from this well, used to go in the water in, in the kitchen, you know, in bathtub, you know.
And we had, like, should be in 50 years later.
[LAUGHTER]
Oh, yes, electricity.
We had all.
Telephone, we had, too.
Yeah, we had because-- not everybody had, though.
But we had-- first thing, we was in business.
And the other thing is we was comfortable, you know, with, with finances.
No, we didn't drive a car.
No.
We used to [SIGHS] go around-- they used to have in the city, you know, like, taxis.
They had a few, not too many.
And they had horses with, like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what they call those-- horse and buggies--
--but then, like, for passenger, you know?
And they used to call in.
And they used to come in and take you.
And we used to walk a lot.
Used to walk a lot.
[BACKROUND NOISE] And we used to have trains and subways.
Yeah.
In this town is-- in this town, they didn't have a subway.
But I was in all kinds cities.
See, I had in Kovno.
Kovno is the capital in this time.
They had already subways, and they had buses, you know.
You used to go each 5-10 minutes, yeah.
In my town was living 35,000.
9,000 Jews.
In public school, I didn't go because for Jewish people, it's hard to go in there.
Maybe in the whole public school maybe was two, three people.
Oh, we had-- the Jewish community had a-- a gymnasium they call this, a high school.
And they had professional, beautiful teachers, you know, from Switzerland and from Germany, you know?
And they supervised-- not supervised-- the Lithuanian government education--
--department used to control this, you know.
They didn't support us.
But they controlled us, you know.
When they-- when we had the examens, they used to come in--
--you know, and check if everything goes right.
And they had-- they used to even send out-- the controllers, they used to send out in Jerusalem to teach them the Hebrew language because everything was in Hebrew.
The--
Yeah.
So they would send the non-Jewish controllers to Jerusalem.
To Jerusalem in a-- in a university.
And they used to graduate for the Hebrew language.
And after this, they used to come in.
They used to control the, the--
--Jewish gymnasium.
Yeah, I remember bar mitzvah, yeah.
The bar--
The bar mitzvah-- beside, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] beside in, in the gymnasium, they teach us religion, you know, the Bible, and the Tanakh, and all kind stuff.
When-- on the end, after school, my father used to send me to a, a rabbi, a private rabbi, for teaching, you know, this Judaic study.
And before bar mitzvah, they had a student-- we used to go to became a, a rabbi.
They had special places where people go to became rabbis.
And it's not like in United States, four years.
They used to go there by 20 years.
[LAUGHTER]
So he used to teach me, you know-- Mhm.
--how to do the bar mitzvah, yeah.
No, no, no.
Yeah, just in the house.
We didn't have in this time hotels, you know.
Uh-huh.
My father invited the, the synagogue's members, you know, who was in, in the synagogue in this Saturday.
He invite in the house, and-- Did you have that-- your bar mitzvah-- in your father's synagogue?
Did he have it then?
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
We was close to all of them, you know?
But one thing is I was close to the youngest one, very close.
Was--
Yeah.
Well, he's now in Israel.
He was in California.
Israel.
No.
We was-- before the war, we didn't have too much trouble between the Jews and non-Jews because the president [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of Lithuania was very good to Jewish people.
He was elected president, but it's not like in United States.
The mayors of the cities used to elect him.

Oh, but the only thing, when they established Lithuania in 1918 after the First World War, the United Nation declared all the countries was occupied by Russian, they used to be released-- that they released Lithuania, too.
And they made a, a government there.
And this president was President Antanas Smetona.
He was there for six months.
And after six months, somebody took over the government, like now in South America, you know.
He took over the government.
And he was, like, a little bit communism, you know, like a socialist, you know.
He took it over.
He took it over.
But he didn't do nothing to him.
He released him, just took away the job from him when they took him out.
So this man who they threw over, Smetona, he was acquainted with a Jewish-- two brothers.
Their name was [NON-ENGLISH].
Mhm.
They was contractors.
And they was comfortable people, rich people, and they knew him-- that when they threw him out from his position, they gave him a beautiful home.
And they supported him with his family.
And after a while, he took over the government back from this guy who took over from him.
And he became president.
And he was president all the time till the Russian come over in 1940 when they took over the country.
And I saw even when he left-- my store was across from his palace.
And I saw his family and cars, they left for the German border.
And from-- Germany let him in because they had agreement with the Russian government when they took it over to let him out, you know.
And they let them out.
And he come over in Germany.
And after from Germany, he left for United States.
And he was living in Chicago.
And in Chicago was a fire.
And in fire, he was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] burned up in fire.
Yeah.
So because of those Jews being nice to him, then he--
Yeah.
--was good to the Jews?
Yeah, he was, yeah.
When even he president, these Jews, these two, they helped him a lot.
Well, this is not official.
But we know he's-- they helped him in all kind advice, you know.
And, and he was very nice to Jewish people.
We used to even call them [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] our father.
Yeah, we-- well, in his-- in our town, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the president's wife's sister [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was the wife from our mayor from the city where I was to-- [LAUGHS]
[LAUGHS]
I don't know how I remember this.
I don't know.
Why do-- I remember things when I was two years old.
Everything.
I don't know.
I don't know.
But I did-- because my brother wrote me a letter, a couple-- I don't know, a couple months ago from Israel.
He asked me something that I wrote him, that he say, how could you remember?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
In this time, I was a child.
He was very little.
I don't know nothing about.
Oh, but you wasn't too much older than I am.
And you remember this?
How could you?
And I don't know how to tell him, you know.
Do you remember if you had any friends who were not Jewish?
Yeah, we had little friends to-- yeah, we had in the business, we also had a lot of friends--
--you know.
We was a, a big corporation in, in Lithuania.
We were doing a lot of business with all kinds, with the government, you know.
We used to make coop for the government.
Yeah.
But this was when I was going even in the-- the gymnasium school. you know.
So in spare time, we used to come in, you know, and they used to teach us how to help people, you know.
And we used to go out on outing, you know, like in the woods sometime for camping, you know, stuff like that.
And they used to always teach us how to help people, you know, older people and young people, you know, in case of disaster.
This is a--
Yeah, we had a uniform.
Green.
Yeah, they used to give badges, too.
I don't know if I had a badge [? this time. ?]
Well, it was quite a few.
I do not remember how many, you know.
If I would tell you, I have to just guess it, you know?
Yeah, we had quite a few, you know, from Jewish kids, you know.
We had a-- we used to always-- together, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we used to spend time a lot in school, too, you know, in spare time and this.
No.
I, I, I wasn't in this.
One time, they took me in a play where I didn't do nothing there, like a storekeeper.
They made like a little store, you know?
They was on the stage, and I like a-- like a storekeeper.
[LAUGHS]
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- I, I have no idea because when I grew up, I didn't have my grandparents.
Uncles-- we had uncles in a different state, next to it, Latvia, if you heard.
You heard Latvia.
There used to be a city, Dvinsk, because before the war, my father was in this city.
My father married in, in Dvinsk.
His wife is from Dvinsk.
And he was living in Dvinsk.
Yeah.
See.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Since the uncles-- all of them was there.
And it's-- years ago, when you used to go from one country to another one, you had to have a passport, like you go from here.
It was difficult, you know?
Well, my father used to go once in a while.
Once in a year, I think, he used to go to his rabbi, you know.
See, like in United State, they got a rabbi, you know, where they-- he's leader, you know.
So before holiday, he used to go.
He used to bless them-- Mhm.
--you know [LAUGHS] And he used to bring the blessing to the family.
[LAUGHS]
Of the war?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The war started in Poland and Germany.
And we was on the border.
But I think this got nothing to do with Lithuania.
The, the Russian took over Lithuania.
It's the same like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sometime a storm comes in here.
So they know a couple-odd hours before.
We didn't know nothing.
What happened-- how they took over Lithuania is the Russian, Stalin's government, called in the Secretary of State of Lithuania when he was in Moscow.
And in the same time when he was there, the tanks from Russia crossed the border.
It's next to it.
And they occupied Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia in a few hours, you know.
We didn't know they will come in--
--see.
We, we didn't know this.
No.
Maybe we had an idea because, you see, when the war started, Poland and Germany-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so after the First World War, they proclaimed Lithuania a country.
And the capital was Vilna for hundreds or thousands of years.

But after the war, when they proclaimed Poland and all the countries independent-- and Lithuania, it's a general from Poland, without saying to nobody, he just-- in with his army when he's occupied Vilna, the capital.
So Lithuania didn't have a capital for a long time, for 25 years, maybe, 28 years.
They didn't have diplomatic relation.
You couldn't send even a letter, even it's next door because they was in a rift, you know.
After this, Poland gave ultimatum to Lithuania.
After 28 years, if you don't make diplomatic relation with us, we will occupy.
So they made it.
They couldn't fight 2 million against 45 million.
When the war started, Hitler with Stalin made the agreement they will split Poland.
So Russia took off a big part of Poland-- Ukraine, they call this.
And they took over Vilna.
When they took over Vilna, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they called up the Lithuanian government.
And they say, you didn't have Vilna never.
And we got now Vilna.
We like to give you a present-- Vilna.
And you can come in and take over.
We'll give you over.
So Lithuania mobilized all the recruits and the ex-soldiers, you know, just to take it over without war, the, the city.
It was five miles from, from, from the border where they are now.
They come over.
The Russian hold them for three months near the border, and they didn't let them in.
Their excuse is they didn't say nobody because they cleaned out the city Vilna.
They took out, out even the knobs from the doors.
When the city was cleared, then they let in the-- [LAUGHS] --the army, the Lithuanian army to take it over.
Then they took it over.
So, but after they took it over, they made bases there--
--see?
They made bases.
Lithuania couldn't refuse nothing because you cannot fight Russia.
Russia is 350 million people.
So the Russians made bases there.
Yeah, the Russians made bases, and they was there.
So we had-- in Panevezys, we had a rabbi.
Oh, but this rabbi was a elected member of the Lithuanian parliament even, see?
And he wasn't too much in the city Panevezys.
He was most in Africa or in the United State.
And he used to go between the Jewish population and collect money.

And with the money he used to send over in Panevezys in the city to hold on the-- all the Jewish institutions-- a hospital and old-age homes and orphans' homes-- to supply money to cover the expense, you know.
For holiday, they used to just come in back to the city, you know.
Oh, but this was good for the city.
Well, one time-- and in Lithuania, it's like this.
A rabbi is not like here.
Each synagogue got a rabbi.
Each temple got a rabbi.
He was the rabbi of all the city.
There was 16 synagogues there.
And he was the rabbi.
And he was in his synagogue.
He didn't go all of them, you know.
But one time when he come over, and they'd made the bases there--
--the Russian.
So he made a speech in, in synagogue to the people.
And he say, today, he say, Russia gave Vilna to Lithuania.
But in a few days later, in a short while, for interest, United States will have to pay with the whole country.
They will take the whole country.
So he--
